Competition

SAMPE Fuselage Competition

University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign | Manufacturing Lead | Spring 2025

→ <2% void content, first-article success

Team with the finished fuselage at the MTS testing machine

What I Built

Manufactured a composite fuselage cylinder (24"×6" OD) capable of withstanding 1,000 lbf in three-point bending using vacuum bagging and autoclave cure.

The Challenge: Meet aerospace-grade quality (<1" deflection, <2% voids) with university lab constraints, fiberglass-only materials, and four structural cutouts—all in a single semester with no room for iteration.

My Role: Manufacturing process development, FMEA risk analysis, and hands-on fabrication lead.

CAD model - SolidWorks

How I Built It

Process Selection: Evaluated 6 methods; chose vacuum bagging + autoclave for optimal quality-feasibility balance.

Material System: Woven E-glass prepreg (20-ply symmetric) + Nomex honeycomb core

Nomex honeycomb core wrapped on aluminum mandrel

Continuous prepreg blanket layup - the innovative solution

Vacuum-bagged part loaded into autoclave for cure

01

Prepreg layup on aluminum mandrel with debulking every 4-6 plies

02

Two-stage autoclave cure (275°F, 40 psi, 90 min)

03

Waterjet cutouts and finishing

Risk Management: Conducted FMEA identifying vacuum bag leaks as highest risk (RPN=60). Implemented automated leak detection and redundant sealing—zero failures during execution.

The Crisis & Solution

At ply 10 of 14, discovered a 3-inch gap—composite wouldn't close. All plies were cut to identical length; we failed to account for increasing circumference as thickness built up.

The All-Nighter Redesign: Abandoned 14 individual wraps → Created one continuous prepreg blanket with progressive ply lengths → Single-roll application onto mandrel

Result: Eliminated delamination risk, maintained fiber continuity, achieved perfect fit on first attempt. This innovation became our competition highlight.

Testing & Results

Finished carbon fiber fuselage section

MTS machine readout during testing

<2%
Void Content
1st

Back to Experience
Research

Frontal Polymerization Modeling

Autonomous Materials Systems Lab, UIUC | Graduate Research Assistant | May – Dec 2024

→ 94% computational cost reduction with manufacturing-relevant accuracy

94% Faster Computation
10% Velocity Accuracy
3°C Temperature Error
12 AMR Levels

The Manufacturing Challenge

Conventional autoclave curing of fiber-reinforced polymer composites (FRPCs) requires 8+ hour cycles consuming hundreds of gigajoules. Frontal polymerization offers a revolutionary alternative—a self-propagating reaction front that cures composites in under 5 minutes with minimal energy input.

The challenge? Predicting front behavior in carbon fiber composites where anisotropic thermal properties create complex propagation patterns that can cause defects if not properly controlled.

My Computational Approach

Working under Professor Philippe H. Geubelle, I developed and compared three modeling strategies using the MOOSE finite element framework:

📐

Level-Set (Uniform Speed)

Fastest approach—tracks front as implicit surface moving at constant velocity. Runtime: 4 hours vs 3 days for reaction-diffusion. Best for process planning.

🔄

Level-Set (Curvature-Affected)

Adds velocity dependence on front curvature. Captures 10% velocity increase near fiber-resin interfaces where heat accumulates.

🧪

Reaction-Diffusion

Full physics model coupling Arrhenius kinetics with heat transfer. Gold standard accuracy but computationally expensive.

🔬

Adaptive Mesh Refinement

12-level AMR concentrates elements at the reaction front (0.1mm resolution) while using coarse mesh elsewhere. Critical for numerical stability.

Technical Implementation

Model Parameters & Validation

Front Velocity6.4 cm/min
Experimental7.0 cm/min
Peak Temperature~200°C
Fiber Conductivity68× resin
Panel Size900 cm²
FrameworkMOOSE/C++

Manufacturing Trade-off Analysis

My models enabled direct comparison of initiation strategies for a 30×30 cm carbon FRPC panel:

Strategy Cure Time Energy Best For
Single-point 2 min 750 J Energy efficiency
Two-point 1 min 1,500 J Balanced
Through-thickness 30 sec 9,600 J Speed priority

Skills & Tools

MOOSE Framework C++ Development Level-Set Methods Adaptive Mesh Refinement Multiphysics FEA Python Post-Processing ParaView Technical Writing
Back to Projects
Academic

Borosilicate Glass-Ceramic Composites

VNR Vignana Jyothi Institute of Engineering | Project Lead | Jan – May 2023

→ 27% hardness improvement over baseline

Team presenting research findings at the departmental "Show and Tell" symposium

What I Built

I developed borosilicate-alumina-copper oxide glass-ceramic composites to address vial breakage in pharmaceutical packaging. As Project Lead, I designed, fabricated, and characterized 24 composite formulations using powder metallurgy techniques, achieving up to 27% hardness improvement while maintaining commercial viability.

This undergraduate thesis resulted in a 52-page technical report and was presented at the departmental research symposium.

How I Built It

Sintered pellets: (a) Pure BS, (b) BS+Al₂O₃, (c) BS+n-Cu₂O, (d) BS+Al₂O₃+n-Cu₂O

⚗️

Powder Metallurgy Process

Established complete workflow: precision weighing (±0.01g), wet grinding with 2% PVA binder, hydraulic compaction at 50 kgf, and controlled sintering in muffle furnace.

🔥

Thermal Processing

Programmed multi-stage profile: 300°C hold (20min) for binder burnout, 5°C/min ramp to 850°C, isothermal sintering (30min), controlled cooling to prevent thermal shock.

The Critical Challenge

Problem: Preventing cristobalite formation during sintering—this crystalline phase causes catastrophic volume changes and cracking.

Solution: Introduced alumina as a network modifier. Al³⁺ ions diffuse into the glass structure, increasing viscosity and suppressing cristobalite nucleation. XRD analysis confirmed amorphous structure preservation.

Characterization Results

Micro Vickers Hardness: (a) BS/Al₂O₃ composites (b) BS/n-Cu₂O composites

XRD patterns confirming amorphous structure across compositions

27%
Hardness Increase
698.66 HV
Peak Hardness
24
Samples Tested
52
Page Thesis

Optimal Composition

Base Glass90% BS
Reinforcement10% Al₂O₃
Hardness698.66 HV
Improvement+27%

Competition

Electric Solar Vehicle Championship

Team Solarians 4.0 | Manufacturing Lead & Brake/Suspension Specialist | 2019-2023

→ 7th Place Nationally – 608.8 points

Team Solarians' solar-electric vehicle competing at ESVC 2023 National Championship

What I Built

I led manufacturing and brake/suspension systems for a 30-member team building a solar-electric three-wheeler for the national Electric Solar Vehicle Championship. Over 4 years, I progressed from team member to Manufacturing Lead, coordinating the complete fabrication workflow—chassis welding, hydraulic brake integration, suspension mounting, and final vehicle assembly.

The vehicle featured a custom tubular steel chassis, independent front suspension, solar panel array, and regenerative braking system designed to maximize efficiency on the competition course.

How I Built It

PVC pipe mockup for driver ergonomics validation

📐

Prototype Testing

Built full-scale PVC pipe mockup to validate driver ergonomics before committing to metal fabrication. Iteratively adjusted seating position, pedal reach, steering wheel placement, and visibility angles with the actual driver.

🔧

Brake System Design

Adapted motorcycle disc brake components (203mm rotors, twin-piston calipers) with custom mounting brackets. Implemented 70/30 front/rear pressure distribution for optimal stopping power.

⚙️

Suspension Engineering

Redesigned mounting brackets when 2023 rulebook changes interfered with original attachment points. Preserved 100mm wheel travel while meeting new compliance requirements.

🏭

Manufacturing Coordination

Led fabrication across welding, machining, and assembly stations. Implemented three-stage inspection protocol ensuring quality control for 30 team members working simultaneously.

Competition Results

7th
Place Nationally

Mechanical Failures

Leadership Growth

This project spanned my entire undergraduate career (2019-2023), giving me the opportunity to grow from a junior team member learning basic fabrication to Manufacturing Lead coordinating 30 people across multiple workstations.

Key lesson: Zero mechanical failures during competition wasn't luck—it was the result of systematic quality control processes I implemented, including the three-stage inspection protocol that caught issues before they reached final assembly.

Competition

SAE Aero Design Challenge

Team Ethereal Predators | CFD Analysis Specialist & Manufacturing Planner | Aug 2021 – Mar 2022

→ 85% CFD accuracy correlation with theoretical calculations

Radio-controlled cargo aircraft designed for SAE Aero Design Challenge

What I Built

I conducted comprehensive CFD analysis and developed manufacturing plans for a 7-member team designing a radio-controlled cargo aircraft. The aircraft featured a 92-inch tapered wing design optimized for maximum payload capacity while meeting strict competition weight and performance requirements.

My primary responsibility was airfoil selection and validation—I analyzed multiple high-lift airfoils using XFLR5 at Reynolds number 385,000, ultimately selecting the SELIG 1223 for its superior lift characteristics (CL max ~2.3) and favorable stall behavior.

How I Did It

📊

Airfoil Analysis

Compared 5 high-lift airfoils (SELIG 1223, E423, RG-15, CH10, FX74) using XFLR5. Evaluated CL, CD, and L/D ratio across angle of attack range to select optimal configuration.

🔬

CFD Validation

Validated airfoil selection using ANSYS Fluent with tetrahedral mesh (2mm elements), k-epsilon turbulence model, and 250 SIMPLE iterations. Achieved 85% correlation with theoretical predictions.

🛠️

Manufacturing Plan

Developed complete balsa wood construction workflow: wing rib cutting, spar placement, leading/trailing edge assembly, servo integration, and MonoKote covering process.

⚖️

CG & Stability

Performed center of gravity analysis to ensure stable flight characteristics. Calculated component placement for optimal balance with varying payload configurations.

Technical Results

85%
CFD Accuracy
92"
Wingspan
2.3
CL Max
16.73m
Takeoff Distance

CFD Analysis Summary

Lift Coefficient (CL)1.4
Drag Coefficient (CD)0.1004
Cruise Lift66.7 N
Cruise Drag5.4 N
Max Lift168.56 N
Reynolds Number385,000

Lessons Learned

Technical Growth: Self-taught ANSYS Fluent CFD methodology during this project. The meshing techniques, turbulence modeling, and post-processing skills I developed here were directly applied to my graduate research on composite materials.

Project Management Reality: Despite strong technical progress, budget constraints ($1,500+ needed for materials and components) forced a pivot to the ESVC competition which had secured sponsorship. This experience taught me the critical importance of securing resources and stakeholder buy-in before deep technical investment.

Skills Demonstrated

ANSYS Fluent XFLR5 CFD Meshing k-epsilon Turbulence Airfoil Selection Balsa Construction CG Analysis Technical Documentation

Industry

Quality Assurance & MRB Coordinator

Tata Advanced Systems Ltd | Intern | Oct 2022 – Mar 2023

→ 450+ aerospace components inspected for GE Aerospace & Boeing programs

450+ Parts Inspected
22% Cycle Time Reduction
15%→3% Defect Rate
₹2.5L Scrap Recovered

Role Progression

1

QC Inspector (Months 1-3)

Dimensional inspection using CMM (0.02mm accuracy), surface roughness testing, and GD&T interpretation. Achieved 100% report accuracy with zero customer escapes on GE/Boeing programs.

2

MRB Coordinator (Months 4-6)

Promoted to lead cross-functional Material Review Board meetings. Coordinated disposition decisions between quality, engineering, and production for nonconforming parts.

Key Achievements

🔍

Root Cause Analysis

Identified CNC tool wear as the source of recurring position tolerance violations affecting 15% of batches. Implemented tool life tracking system using SPC, reducing defect rate to under 3%.

Impact: ₹80K monthly savings
📋

Supplier FMEA

Led FMEA investigation when supplier delivered aluminum alloy below specification, affecting 200+ parts. Negotiated use-as-is disposition with engineering based on stress analysis.

Impact: ₹2.5L scrap recovery
⚡

Process Optimization

Streamlined MRB documentation workflow using 8D methodology. Reduced average nonconformance cycle time by 22% through standardized root-cause templates.

Impact: 22% faster resolution

Working with Aerospace Standards

This internship gave me hands-on experience with the rigorous quality requirements of aerospace manufacturing. Every part I inspected was destined for GE Aerospace jet engines or Boeing aircraft—there's no margin for error when lives depend on your work.

The progression from inspector to MRB coordinator taught me that quality isn't just about catching defects—it's about building systems that prevent them.

Skills & Certifications

CMM Operation GD&T (ASME Y14.5) 8D Problem-Solving FMEA Statistical Process Control Root Cause Analysis AS9100/ISO 9001 MRB Coordination
Back to Experience
Industry

Manufacturing Process Intern

EQIC Dies & Moulds Engineers Pvt. Ltd. | Jun – Aug 2022

→ Documented complete die manufacturing lifecycle from design to delivery

12 Processes Documented
HPDC Die Specialty
3 Month Duration
1 Complete Die Tracked

Company Background

EQIC is a precision tooling supplier specializing in High-Pressure Die Casting (HPDC) dies for automotive and aerospace clients including Lucas TVS, Hyundai, and Mahindra. Their dies produce aluminum components for engine blocks, transmission housings, and structural parts.

What I Learned

I tracked a UNIRAC die through its complete manufacturing lifecycle, documenting each process stage:

01

Design & CAM

Die design review, parting line optimization, cooling channel layout, CAM programming for CNC operations

02

CNC Machining

Rough and finish milling of die blocks (P20 tool steel), tolerance control to ±0.02mm on critical surfaces

03

Heat Treatment

Hardening and tempering cycles to achieve 48-52 HRC, stress relieving to prevent distortion

04

EDM & Finishing

Wire EDM for complex geometries, sinker EDM for cavities, surface grinding and polishing

05

Assembly & Tryout

Die assembly, alignment verification, trial shots to validate fill pattern and part quality

Key Takeaway

This internship gave me appreciation for the precision required in tooling manufacturing. A single die represents hundreds of hours of skilled work—and any defect gets replicated thousands of times in production parts.

Understanding die manufacturing helps me design composite parts that are actually manufacturable, not just theoretically optimal.

Skills Gained

Die Design Principles CNC Machining Wire/Sinker EDM Heat Treatment HPDC Process Process Documentation GD&T Reading Shop Floor Communication
